summary: I met Muriel MacGregor in the fall of 1961 when I moved to Estes Park. She was a soft-spoken woman who kept cattle on her property near Black Canyon. Despite many stories about her, like carrying dead calves in her car, I found her to be kind and lonely, reaching out for friendship. Over the years, especially after her stroke around 1949, I helped her with daily tasks and took her to the chiropractor in Loveland. She loved visiting with my grandchildren and cared deeply for her cattle, often driving around the ranch to check on them.

I moved to Estes Park in October 1961 and lived near Black Canyon Ranch. Estes Park was where I met Muriel MacGregor and where much of my story unfolded. The town had places like Riverside Market near Fun City and was close to areas like Mary's Lake and High Drive where I eventually bought a house. Estes Park was a community with ranches, restaurants, and a close-knit feel, especially during the 1960s when my husband was building condos nearby.

When I first arrived in Estes Park in 1961, we lived at Black Canyon Ranch, which was actually a dude ranch with horses and cabins. My husband was building condos there during that winter. The ranch was near the Black Canyon Restaurant and was surrounded by the MacGregor property. It was a beautiful place overlooking Black Canyon and a hub for local ranching and community life.

The Black Canyon Restaurant was located about half a mile from where we lived on the Black Canyon Ranch in 1961. It was part of the dude ranch setup with horses and cabins. The MacGregor property surrounded the area around the restaurant, and it was a familiar landmark for me as I often passed by there on my way to visit Muriel MacGregor or check on the ranches.

Dr. Curry Myers owned property near the Black Canyon Ranch where we lived in 1961. His property was surrounded by the MacGregor land, and the area was known as Black Canyon Hills. Though I didn't interact much with Dr. Myers personally, his land was a reference point in the community and close to where my husband was building condos.

Evergreen Lane was a street near the MacGregor Ranch and just across from the entrance to the Black Canyon Restaurant. In the early 1960s, I saw an ad for a house to rent there and contacted Muriel MacGregor about it. Although the house was too small for us, it was clear Muriel wanted us to rent it badly, showing her loneliness and desire for friendship. Evergreen Lane was a convenient location close to my husband's work and the ranch.

Highway 36 was mentioned as a nearby route when discussing Evergreen Lane and the general area around Black Canyon and Estes Park. It served as a geographic reference point for locations like Otie Whiteside's and the MacGregor Ranch. While I didn't live directly on Highway 36, it was part of the local road network connecting the ranches and homes in the area.

After deciding the house on Evergreen Lane was too small, we bought a home on High Drive in Estes Park. This was around the winter of 1961-1962, and it gave us more room for our family, including my mother-in-law. High Drive was a comfortable place to live while I continued visiting Muriel MacGregor and managing life around the ranches.

Mary's Lake was near where Muriel MacGregor had a cabin, and it was the place where she had a fall on the ice that led to her stroke around 1949. She told me about this incident, which affected her mobility for the rest of her life. The lake area was part of the broader Estes Park region where many ranches and cabins were located.

Loveland was where I took Muriel MacGregor to see a chiropractor since Estes Park didn’t have one at the time. This was in the mid-1960s, around 1964-1965, to help with her pain from the stroke and hip injury. Loveland was important for her medical care and relief during those years.

During the mid-1960s, my son was preparing to go to Vietnam, and Muriel MacGregor kept up with the news about the war. She often talked about him and showed concern, which was a way she connected with me and my family. The Vietnam War was a backdrop to our lives at that time, adding a layer of worry and connection.

Greeley was where two of my grandsons lived while I had them visiting in the summer. Muriel MacGregor enjoyed having the grandchildren around, including those from Greeley. It was part of the extended family connections that brought joy to her in her later years.

Fish Creek was one of the pasture properties Muriel MacGregor owned near Estes Park. She would spend hours driving around Fish Creek and other pastures like McGraw Ranch and John Hayden's property to check on her cattle. It was part of her daily routine despite her limited mobility after her stroke.

McGraw Ranch was another property near Estes Park that belonged to Muriel MacGregor. She would drive around there to watch her cattle, even though she was limited physically by her stroke. The ranch was part of the network of lands she managed and cared for deeply.

John Hayden owned property near Estes Park that was adjacent to lands Muriel MacGregor managed. She included his property in her regular rounds to check on cattle. Though I didn’t know John Hayden personally, his land was part of the area Muriel cared for.

Riverside Market, formerly known as Richardson’s, was the grocery store near Fun City in Estes Park that delivered groceries to Muriel MacGregor. They provided delivery service for at least three years until her death, which was a great help since she couldn’t shop for herself. The delivery men, including Barney and J. Bruns, were familiar faces and friends of my family.

Fun City was a landmark near Riverside Market in Estes Park where Muriel MacGregor got her groceries delivered. It helped me describe the location of the market and the general area where Muriel lived and received support. Fun City was part of the community fabric in Estes Park during the 1960s and 70s.

Virginia Denning knew Muriel MacGregor and her family well. Her parents rented the Quiner House on the ranch for about ten summers, and Virginia often talked about the Boyce children, Muriel’s maiden family. Though Virginia was married and no longer living on the ranch when I arrived, she was part of the local history connected to Muriel.

The Boyce family was Muriel MacGregor’s maiden family, and they had strong ties to the ranch near Estes Park. Virginia Denning, whose parents rented a cabin on the ranch, often spoke about the Boyce children. The family was part of the local community history and connected to the Quiner House where they spent many summers.

The Quiner House was a cabin on the ranch near Estes Park rented by Virginia Denning’s parents for about ten summers. It was part of the local ranch community connected to the Boyce family, Muriel MacGregor’s maiden family. The house represented the seasonal life and history of families who spent time on the ranch.
